title: Getting started with an e-spinner
subtitle: Setting up and using an electric spinning wheel for the first time
excerpt: An e-spinner (electric spinning wheel) uses a motor in place of a treadle to drive the flyer and bobbin. The advantages are consistent speed, portability, and low physical demand, useful for people with mobility limitations or for travel spinning. The technique is identical to treadle spinning; the adjustment is in controlling motor speed rather than treadle rhythm.
sourceNotes: E-spinner technique is an established subject in contemporary spinning instruction. Observation-led.

content.text: runs identically to a treadle wheel in every respect except that the motor replaces foot power. The flyer, orifice, bobbin, and drive band work the same way. The main operational adjustment is learning to control
by dial rather than by treadle pressure.

attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing away from the flyer and drive band. Switch the motor off before changing bobbins or threading the orifice.
attrs.title: Wheel safety

items.name: E-spinner with at least one bobbin
Prepared roving or top for practice

content.text: Step 1: Thread the leader and set speed

Thread a leader (short length of scrap yarn) through the orifice and tie it to the bobbin. Set the motor speed to its lowest setting. Turn the motor on and check that the bobbin takes up the leader without pulling it too fast. Ideal take-up speed for DK: the leader feeds onto the bobbin at roughly the same pace as normal walking.
Step 2: Attach the fibre and begin drafting

Attach the roving to the leader. Begin drafting with whichever method suits the fibre preparation. The e-spinner's consistent speed helps maintain even twist insertion; the main learning is matching drafting speed to motor speed. Draft slower or faster until the twist entering the yarn looks even.
Step 3: Adjust speed for yarn weight

Faster motor speed inserts more twist per length of drafted fibre, useful for fine yarn and lace. Slower speed inserts less twist, appropriate for bulky yarn and woollen-draw work. On most e-spinners, the flyer ratios are also swappable (like interchangeable whorls on a treadle wheel); use a higher-ratio whorl for fine spinning, a lower ratio for bulky.

attrs.intro: Two common problems on first use of an e-spinner.

items.fix: Reduce take-up by loosening the drive-band tension or moving to a whorl with a lower ratio. The yarn should feed onto the bobbin without resistance from the spinner's hand.
items.cause: Take-up tension too high: the bobbin is pulling the yarn in faster than it is being spun.
items.symptom: Yarn keeps breaking at the orifice

items.fix: Check drive band tension and re-seat it. Wind the yarn off onto the niddy-noddy if the bobbin is above two-thirds full.
items.cause: Bobbin is overfull or the drive band has slipped.

attrs.heading: Common problems
